fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the Markdown processing toolkit we've been scoping out. The core idea is that devs should be able to throw Markdown text at it and get back HTML, plain text, or structured tree info without wiring up their own parsers or regex nightmares. We talked about this in the last planning sync — basically the same philosophy as what we did with the config sanitizer, where the adapter layer keeps all the messy I/O stuff away from the actual business logic.

Some specific things that came up: people need to be able to turn on/off individual syntax extensions (tables, strikethrough, that kind of thing), handle fancy quote/apostrophe conversion for publication-style output, and also do things like walk the document tree or poke at individual node properties. There was also a request from the content team about being able to edit the tree before rendering — like inserting or moving chunks around programmatically.

One thing I keep hearing from the dev side is that error messages right now are too implementation-specific and break when we switch runtimes. We need errors to come back in a normalized, language-neutral format so the frontend can handle them consistently regardless of what blows up underneath.

The input/output contract needs to be stable and JSON-based for the adapter layer. Make sure the round-trip serialization story is solid too — that one came up in QA last sprint. Roughly 13 features total per the scope doc.

trigger_keywords: how should plain string input be handled
what happens when input is not an object
string vs object input format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the adapter input is a plain JSON string (not an object), it should be treated as raw Markdown text and rendered to HTML using default options — no extensions, no flags. When the input is a JSON object, it must have at minimum a 'markdown' key. If the input type is neither a string nor a valid object with a markdown key, the system must return error=invalid_input_type.

trigger_keywords: how do hard line breaks work
what option enables br tags inside paragraphs
hardbreak rendering flag name
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Hard line break rendering is enabled via the HARDBREAKS render option (mapped to the :hardbreaks symbol in the Ruby layer). When enabled, a newline inside a paragraph in the source Markdown is rendered as a self-closing
tag followed by a newline, rather than being collapsed into a space. The feature2 test case confirms the output format:

foo
baz

.

trigger_keywords: smart quotes option name
how to enable typographic punctuation
smart punctuation parse flag
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Smart punctuation is a PARSE-time option, not a render-time option. It is enabled via the SMART parse flag (`:smart` in Ruby). It converts straight double quotes to curly “”, straight single quotes/apostrophes to curly ‘’, and nested quote patterns accordingly. It must NOT be passed as a render flag — doing so is an invalid input and should return error=invalid_input_type with selector=html_parse_flag_not_allowed.

trigger_keywords: which extensions are supported
how to pass extension names
valid extension identifiers
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Supported extension names include: 'table', 'strikethrough', 'autolink', 'tagfilter', 'tasklist', 'footnotes'. They are passed as an array of strings under the 'extensions' key in the input object. Extensions not in this list should be silently ignored or cause a graceful no-op. The strikethrough extension uses double-tilde (~~text~~) syntax. Enabling 'table' enables GFM-style pipe tables.

trigger_keywords: fenced code metadata attribute name
how is the info string after the language rendered
data attribute for code block metadata
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a fenced code block has an info string with a first word (language) followed by additional content, the language becomes the class='language-X' on the <code> tag, and the remaining info string content (everything after the first word) is emitted as data-meta="...". If there is no metadata beyond the language, the data-meta attribute is omitted entirely. This behavior requires the sourcepos or metadata rendering option to be active in the renderer.

trigger_keywords: footnote extension name
how are footnotes enabled
footnote backlink unicode character
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Footnotes are enabled via the 'footnotes' extension string. The rendered output wraps the footnotes section in
, uses an
    for the list, and each item gets id='fn{N}'. The reference in the body is a 1. The backlink uses the Unicode character ↩ (U+21A9) and has class='footnote-backref'.

trigger_keywords: task list html output format
checkbox rendering attributes
how to get task item checked states
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Task list items render as <input type="checkbox" disabled="" /> for unchecked and <input type="checkbox" checked="" disabled="" /> for checked items. The 'view' key in the input object controls output: 'html' returns the full HTML, 'states' returns a comma-separated list of 'checked' or 'unchecked' labels for each item, one per task item in document order. The tasklist extension must be enabled for this parsing to occur.

trigger_keywords: plain text output format for lists
how are tables rendered in plaintext mode
what does the plaintext renderer produce
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Plain text output uses commonmarker's built-in plaintext/commonmark rendering path. Ordered list items are formatted as 'N.  text' (two spaces after the dot). Unordered list items are indented with two spaces and use a dash: '  - text'. Tables are serialized with pipe syntax: '| a | b |' with a separator row '| --- | --- |'. Inline emphasis markers (* and _) are stripped. Paragraphs are separated by blank lines.

trigger_keywords: how does round trip serialization work
what format is the serialized markdown output
what keys does the roundtrip JSON response contain
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The round-trip feature parses the input Markdown, renders it to HTML (original_html), serializes the parsed AST back to Markdown using the commonmark formatter (serialized_markdown), then reparses that serialized Markdown and renders it again (reparsed_html). The output is a single JSON object with exactly three keys: 'original_html', 'serialized_markdown', and 'reparsed_html', followed by a trailing newline. The table extension must be enabled for the round-trip to include table HTML.

trigger_keywords: how to walk the document tree
what does walk_types return
difference between walk and child traversal views
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'walk_types' view performs a full depth-first walk of the document AST and returns a comma-separated string of node type names in traversal order (e.g., 'document,paragraph,text,emph,text,text'). The 'child_types' view returns only the direct children of the first paragraph node, not the full tree. Node type names are lowercase strings matching the commonmarker node type enum names (e.g., 'document', 'paragraph', 'text', 'emph', 'strong', 'heading', 'code', 'list', 'item').

trigger_keywords: what node attributes can be read and written
how to update a link url
which attributes does the heading node expose
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Supported node attribute kinds and their fields: 'link' exposes url and title (new_url to update); 'image' exposes url and title (new_title to update); 'heading' exposes level (new_level to update); 'list' exposes list_type (bullet or ordered), start (ordered start number), and tight (boolean); 'code_block' exposes fence_info string. Output format is 'key=value' lines, followed by 'updated_key=new_value' lines for any updated fields.

trigger_keywords: what tree mutations are supported
how does insert_empty_paragraph_before work
what edits can be applied to the AST
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Supported edit values for the 'edit' key: 'insert_empty_paragraph_before' inserts a new empty paragraph node before the first paragraph in the document; 'insert_empty_paragraph_after' appends a new empty paragraph node after the first paragraph; 'prepend_text' prepends an inline text node to the first paragraph's children; 'append_text' appends an inline text node; 'replace_emph_text' replaces the text inside the first emph node; 'unwrap_emph' removes the emph node and hoists its text child into the parent. After mutation, the full document is rendered to HTML.

trigger_keywords: what are the normalized error categories
how are errors reported in the output
what does invalid_input_type error look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All errors are reported as key=value lines, never as exceptions or JSON error objects. The format is always three lines: 'error=<category>', 'context=normalized_errors', 'selector=<input_selector_value>'. Known error categories: 'invalid_input_type' (input is wrong type, flag used in wrong position, or disallowed option). The selector field echoes back the 'attempt' value from the input object that triggered the error. No stack traces or language-specific error messages should appear in output.

trigger_keywords: adapter separation from core logic
how should stdin and stdout be wired up
what is the IO boundary pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The execution adapter reads a single JSON value from stdin, routes it to the appropriate core domain method based on input shape (string vs object, presence of 'view', 'edit', 'kind', 'extensions', 'attempt' keys), and writes the result to stdout. The core Markdown engine must have zero knowledge of JSON or IO. All JSON parsing and output formatting lives in the adapter layer. The core exposes idiomatic Ruby methods. This is the same separation pattern used in the config sanitizer adapter from the previous sprint.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same philosophy as what we did with the config sanitizer
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Dependency Inversion Principle pattern where the adapter layer (src/adapter/ or equivalent) handles all JSON stdin/stdout and delegates to pure domain objects in src/core/ — specifically, the constraint that core business logic must never import or reference JSON parsing or IO modules directly.

context_pointers.vague_reference: roughly 13 features total per the scope doc
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Features 1 through 13 as defined in start.md: basic HTML, hard breaks, smart punctuation, extensions, fenced code metadata, footnotes, task lists, plain text, round-trip serialization, tree traversal, node attributes, tree mutation, and normalized errors — each corresponding to a public test case file in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/.

context_pointers.vague_reference: errors to come back in a normalized language-neutral format
context_pointers.actual_meaning:
feature13_normalized_errors.json contract: output is always exactly 'error=<category>
context=normalized_errors
selector=<attempt_value>
' — three key=value lines, no JSON wrapping, no Ruby exception text, and the three known attempt selectors are 'non_text_document', 'bad_html_render_flag_type', and 'html_parse_flag_not_allowed'.

feature9_commonmark_roundtrip.json: the adapter must parse input Markdown with the table extension enabled, render to HTML, serialize AST back to CommonMark Markdown via the commonmark formatter, reparse serialized output, render again, then print a JSON object with keys original_html, serialized_markdown, reparsed_html followed by a newline — matching the exact escaped output in the test case.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but it's not something I have specific detail on from the business side. You'll want to check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference with the feature descriptions in start.md — those together should give you the exact contract. If something still seems ambiguous after that, flag it and we can loop in the tech lead.

feature10_node_tree.json.description: Parsed documents expose stable node traversal and navigation order.

feature11_node_attributes.json.description: Structured document nodes expose and update attributes appropriate to their Markdown construct.

feature12_node_mutation.json.description: Structured document nodes can be inserted, appended, edited, unwrapped, and rendered back to HTML.

feature13_normalized_errors.json.description: Invalid input, unsupported options, and invalid document operations are reported with language-neutral error categories.

feature1_basic_html.json.description: Markdown inline emphasis is rendered as HTML while preserving paragraph structure.

feature2_hardbreak_html.json.description: Line breaks inside a paragraph are rendered as hard HTML breaks when hard-break rendering is requested.

feature3_smart_punctuation.json.description: Smart punctuation rendering converts straight quotes and apostrophes to typographic punctuation.

input.markdown:
"Hello," said the spider.
"'Shelob' is my name."

cases.expected_output: “Hello,” said the spider. “‘Shelob’ is my name.”

| **x** | |

Another extension:

~~hi~~

feature5_fenced_code_metadata.json.description: Fenced code block info strings are rendered as language class and optional metadata when requested.

feature6_footnote_html.json.description: Footnote references and definitions render as linked superscript references and a footnotes section.

Hello1.

  1. Hey! ↩

feature7_task_list.json.description: Task-list items render disabled checkbox inputs and expose checked states.

  • Define task list

- [ ] Define task list

feature8_plaintext.json.description: Rendered documents can be converted to plain text while preserving list and table text layout.

input.markdown:
Hi *there*!

1. I am a numeric list.
2. I continue the list.
* Suddenly, an unordered list!
* What fun!

Okay, _enough_.

| a   | b   |
| --- | --- |
| c   | d   |

cases.expected_output:
Hi there!

1.  I am a numeric list.
2.  I continue the list.

  - Suddenly, an unordered list!
  - What fun!

Okay, enough.

| a | b |
| --- | --- |
| c | d |

feature9_commonmark_roundtrip.json.description: Serializing a parsed document back to Markdown preserves equivalent HTML when parsed again.

cases.input:
Hi *there*!

1. I am a numeric list.
2. I continue the list.
* Suddenly, an unordered list!
* What fun!

Okay, _enough_.

| a   | b   |
| --- | --- |
| c   | d   |
cases.expected_output:
{"original_html":"

Hi there!

  1. I am a numeric list.
  2. I continue the list.
  • Suddenly, an unordered list!
  • What fun!

Okay, enough.

a b
c d
","serialized_markdown":"Hi *there*\! 1. I am a numeric list. 2. I continue the list. - Suddenly, an unordered list\! - What fun\! Okay, *enough*. | a | b | | --- | --- | | c | d | ","reparsed_html":"

Hi there!

  1. I am a numeric list.
  2. I continue the list.
  • Suddenly, an unordered list!
  • What fun!

Okay, enough.

a b
c d
"}
